# Spacefast ‚Äî References

Deep reference for the Spacefast skill. The router and the load-bearing publish path live in
`SKILL.md`; this file holds everything an agent loads only when the task needs it. Nothing here
overrides the safety rules in `SKILL.md`.

## Update A Space

Prefer current local state when present (walk up from the working directory):

- project state: `.spacefast/state.json` (and non-secret `.spacefast/space.json`)
- auth state: `~/.spacefast/auth.json`

Treat state files as private cache only. Never commit them, never show claim tokens, and never tell
the user to inspect state for the claim link or auth status. Persist state after a first publish so
later publishes update the same space (see Save Your State in `SKILL.md`): use mode `0600`, verify
`.spacefast/state.json` is ignored by git, and never include `.spacefast/` in an archive.

To publish a new version of an existing space, send the same `spaceId` with bearer auth ‚Äî
never re-run the first-publish recipe when saved state exists:

```bash
curl -sS -H "Authorization: Bearer <claim-token-or-access-token>" \
  -F 'spaceId=<spc_id>' \
  -F "files=@index.html" \
  https://api.spacefast.com/v1/publish
```

Use the saved claim token for an unclaimed anonymous space, or an access token for owned
spaces. Send a folder as `-F archive=@site.zip` exactly like a first publish; the receipt
shape is identical.

## After The User Claims

Always show the user the claim link (`data.claim.url`) and its expiry (`data.claim.expiresAt`).
If the user says "claimed", check claim status and exchange the saved claim token; do not wait
for a publish failure before continuing.
After the user claims the space in the browser, the saved claim token stops authorizing
publishes: the next publish fails with error code `space_claimed_credential_available`. That
failure is the signal to upgrade your credential ‚Äî call the exchange endpoint exactly once:

```bash
curl -sS -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer <claim-token>" \
  https://api.spacefast.com/v1/anonymous-claim/exchange
```

It returns `{ "data": { "space": {...}, "credential": { "accessToken": "...", "label": "..." } } }`.
Save `data.credential.accessToken` into `.spacefast/state.json` (replace the `claimToken`
field with `"accessToken"`), then retry the publish with the new bearer. The exchange works
exactly once; if it returns `continuation_used` or `continuation_unavailable`, ask the user
to mint an access token in the dashboard (Account ‚Üí Access tokens).

## Manifest Flow

For larger or resumable uploads, use the manifest flow:

1. `POST /v1/publish` with JSON `{ "spaceId": "...", "files": [{ "path", "size", "contentType", "sha256", "sourceUrl" }] }`.
2. For each `data.upload.targets[]`, send `target.method` to `target.url` with `target.headers`.
3. If `target.body.kind` is `"url"`, send JSON `{ "url": target.body.url }`; otherwise send the local file bytes for `target.path`.
4. POST `data.links.finalize`.
5. Share the final `liveUrl`, `immutableUrl`, and diagnostics.

If no files changed, the API may omit `upload`; report the receipt and diagnostics rather than
inventing a separate success signal.

MCP mapping:

- `prepare_publish` creates the manifest upload session and returns signed upload instructions.
- Upload each file to the returned signed URL outside MCP.
- `resume_publish` refreshes the advertised file list or expired upload targets.
- `finalize_publish` completes the version and returns the final publish receipt.

Do not base64 large files into MCP arguments. Use inline `publish.files[]` only for tiny generated
files or cloud clients that truly cannot perform the signed PUT step.

## Import From Link Or Upload

If the user gives you an HTTPS archive URL, use import-from-link instead of downloading and
re-uploading it:

```bash
curl -sS https://api.spacefast.com/v1/spaces/{spaceId}/imports \
  -H "content-type: application/json" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $SPACEFAST_TOKEN" \
  -d '{"source":{"kind":"url","url":"https://example.com/site.zip"}}'
```

In MCP, call `import_from_url`.

If the archive exists only on the agent side, use an upload-sourced import:

1. `POST /v1/spaces/{spaceId}/imports` with JSON `{ "source": { "kind": "upload" } }`, or call
   `prepare_import_upload` in MCP.
2. PUT the archive to `{data.archiveUpload.baseUrl}/{data.archiveUpload.files[0]}` with
   `Authorization: Bearer <data.archiveUpload.token>`.
3. If the token expires, `GET /v1/imports/{importId}` re-mints `archiveUpload`; in MCP call
   `resume_import_upload`.
4. `POST /v1/imports/{importId}/finalize`, or call `finalize_import_upload` in MCP.

Treat archive upload tokens exactly like publish upload tokens. They are short-lived credentials.

## Device Login

Use device login when the user wants owned/permanent publishes and no API key is available.
This does not require the CLI.

Start (`POST /v1/auth/device`):

```bash
curl -sS https://api.spacefast.com/v1/auth/device \
  -H "content-type: application/json" \
  -d '{}'
```

Show the user the returned `data.verificationUrl` and `data.userCode` exactly. Tell them to open
the link, enter/confirm the code if needed, choose the team/preset, and approve.

Poll (`POST /v1/auth/device/poll`) no faster than `data.interval` seconds:

```bash
curl -sS https://api.spacefast.com/v1/auth/device/poll \
  -H "content-type: application/json" \
  -d '{"deviceCode":"<data.deviceCode>"}'
```

Handle poll statuses:

- `pending`: wait `interval` seconds and poll again.
- `approved`: capture `data.apiKey.secret` immediately; it is shown only once.
- `denied`, `expired`, or `consumed`: stop and start a new device flow if the user still wants login.
- `slow_down` or HTTP 429: increase the wait; repeated fast polls can invalidate the code.

For a one-off operation, keep the key in `SPACEFAST_TOKEN` only. Before durable persistence, ask the
user if they want this machine to stay signed in. Do not persist auth by default on CI, shared
hosts, or throwaway agent sandboxes. To save the key for future direct API and CLI-compatible use,
write `~/.spacefast/auth.json` atomically with mode `0600`:

```json
{
  "accessToken": "<api-key-secret>",
  "apiUrl": "https://api.spacefast.com",
  "accessTokenId": "<api-key-id-if-returned>",
  "account": { "id": "<subject-or-user-id>", "email": "<email-if-known>" }
}
```

After approval, always validate saved or in-memory auth with `GET /v1/me`:

```bash
curl -sS https://api.spacefast.com/v1/me -H "Authorization: Bearer $SPACEFAST_TOKEN"
```

For claiming an anonymous space during login, start the device flow with:

```json
{
  "claim": { "spaceId": "spc_...", "claimToken": "<secret-claim-token>" }
}
```

Approval claims the space into the chosen team; the next successful poll includes
`data.claimedSpaceId`.

## CLI

If `sf` is already installed, use it for convenience. Do not install it just to publish once; the
direct API in `SKILL.md` is complete.

```bash
sf publish {file-or-dir}
sf publish {file-or-dir} --json
sf login
sf spaces claim
sf versions
sf rollback {version}
sf setup agent --agent auto --connect
sf setup agent --agent codex
sf mcp install --agent codex --mode hosted --connect
sf mcp install --agent codex --mode local --transport stdio --connect
sf mcp
sf mcp daemon --host 127.0.0.1 --port 3945 --path /mcp
sf mcp status --json
sf mcp http --host 127.0.0.1 --port 3945 --path /mcp
```

The CLI unlocks command sugar for domains, passwords, variables, logs, build/deploy, version
history, rollback, diagnostics, and On-Device MCP serving. `sf setup agent` prints the full integration
ladder; `sf mcp install` prints or writes MCP client config; raw `sf mcp` starts a stdio server,
and `sf mcp daemon` starts reusable local HTTP with a 0600 manifest for trusted local clients.
Use `--json` when another tool needs to parse output.

The command reference is inventory, not permission. Explicit confirmation is required before
destructive or account-wide changes such as API key revocation, DNS mutation, repository mutation,
team member changes, transfers, rollback, deletion, raw signed `sf api` writes, On-Device MCP
`dryRun=false` execution, or workspace writes. Also ask before commands that can reveal secrets or
private content, including environment variable reads, logs, repository archives/files, webhook
payloads, and raw signed API reads. Redact tokens, env values, webhook payloads, logs, and raw
envelopes before sharing.

## MCP

Use the CLI directly when the agent or user is already in a local checkout and just needs to publish,
inspect, roll back, manage domains, or run CI-friendly commands. Use MCP when an agent client needs
structured tools/resources, an approval flow, or bounded file access exposed to that client.
Use hosted MCP for cloud-only clients and On-Device MCP through the CLI when the client needs the
user's checkout, bounded local file access, or locally installed credentials.

Hosted Streamable HTTP endpoint:

```text
https://mcp.spacefast.com
```

The endpoint is also the OAuth resource identifier (`https://mcp.spacefast.com`). Hosted MCP requires an
OAuth access token with `mcp:tools`; the actual tool call still needs the matching API scopes, such
as `teams:read` for team context, `spaces:read` for status/version/log reads, `spaces:write` for
claim/create/update, `publish:write` for publishing and rollback, or `domains:read`/`domains:write`
for domain inventory, DNS diagnostics, assignment, verification, and mutation.
Hosted tools are cloud-safe: `search`, `publish` with inline files or hosted virtual
workspace paths, `prepare_publish`,
`resume_publish`, `finalize_publish`, `import_from_url`, `prepare_import_upload`,
`resume_import_upload`, `finalize_import_upload`, `claim`, `status`, `versions`, `diff`,
`rollback`, `logs`, `build_logs`, `workspace_list/read/write/apply_patch/diff`,
`workspace_shell`, and QuickJS-backed `execute` over that hosted-safe registry. Hosted
MCP must not promise local filesystem access; hosted path-based publish reads only the session
virtual workspace.
Hosted sessions are owner-leased by the server; if an active session lands on the wrong hosted
worker, retry with the returned guidance or initialize a new session after the lease expires. Do
not describe hosted MCP as local-file capable or cold-rehydratable unless the API/docs explicitly
return that capability.

On-Device MCP:

```bash
sf mcp
sf mcp daemon --host 127.0.0.1 --port 3945 --path /mcp
sf mcp status --json
sf mcp http --host 127.0.0.1 --port 3945 --path /mcp
```

Prefer stdio for desktop/terminal coding agents. Use `sf mcp daemon` when a local HTTP MCP client
needs a durable endpoint; it generates or accepts a bearer token and writes
`~/.spacefast/mcp-daemon.json` with mode `0600`. Agents may read that manifest locally, but should not
print the token into chat or logs. If binding Streamable HTTP outside loopback, require
`SPACEFAST_MCP_HTTP_TOKEN` or `--http-token` and restrict `--cors-origin`.

Use `sf mcp install --mode hosted` for a hosted config block and `sf mcp install --mode local`
for On-Device config. Add `--connect` to write the selected client config instead of only printing it.
Use raw `sf mcp` for stdio, `sf mcp daemon` for reusable local Streamable HTTP, and `sf mcp http`
for an explicitly managed one-off HTTP server.

Hosted workspace tools operate on the durable virtual workspace for the MCP session. Use them for
cloud-agent generated files, transformations, handoff between agents, and `publish({ path: "." })`.
Use `workspace_manifest` for cheap inventory, `workspace_export({ includeContent: true })` for a
portable JSON bundle, `workspace_import` for inline handoff files, and `workspace_import_url` when
another system already produced a signed HTTPS bundle. Use `workspace_shell` for bounded just-bash
file and text operations in `/workspace`; network, Python, nested JavaScript, and host filesystem
access are disabled.

On-Device MCP tools include every hosted cloud/API tool plus real-checkout `publish(path)` and
bounded local `workspace_list/read/write/apply_patch/diff`. Treat workspace writes, patching,
rollback, DNS/account mutation, `workspace_shell`, and `dryRun=false` code execution as
approval-requiring actions. Remote ChatGPT/browser-style clients should not need a download; they
should use hosted MCP unless they specifically need the user's real checkout.

For workspace edits, start with `workspace_list` / `workspace_read`, prefer
`workspace_apply_patch` for known text changes, then call `workspace_diff` before reporting. Use
`workspace_write` only for new files or generated assets. Hosted workspace state is virtual and
session-scoped; On-Device workspace state is bounded to the configured local workspace root.
For cross-agent handoff, export the hosted workspace with content, store it at a private HTTPS URL
or pass it inline when small, then import it into the next hosted session before publishing.

`execute` defaults to `dryRun=true`. For multi-step mutating JavaScript, call it with
`dryRun=false` to get a structured `approval_required` checkpoint. Show the preview to the user,
then use the returned `approval.url` when present so the user can approve or deny in the browser.
If the MCP client supports native elicitation, Spacefast may ask for approval inline and resume the
checkpoint without a separate browser trip. After browser approval, call `resume` with
the original `resumeToken`; for non-browser flows call `resume` with
`decision="approve"` or `decision="deny"`. Retried resume calls can replay the settled outcome, but
the resume token is still a short-lived secret capability and must not be printed or persisted.
Spacefast approval is always required for `dryRun=false` code-mode execution; do not invent bypass
flags.
Code runs in the approved QuickJS runtime with raw network disabled. Do not execute generated
JavaScript in the shell just to use Spacefast tools.
Use `search` before code-mode execution. Inside code mode, use `tools.describe({ name })`
for exact argument shape details and `tools.policy({ name })` before direct mutation when approval,
destructive, open-world, or workspace behavior is unclear.

## CI And Agent Accounts

For CI, bots, or durable local runtimes, prefer a dashboard-created agent account that exports a
`SPACEFAST_AGENT_CONFIG` JSON blob and authenticates with private_key_jwt. For one-off jobs,
`SPACEFAST_TOKEN` is acceptable when stored as a masked CI secret. In either case:

- Use `sf publish {output-dir} --json` for static artifacts.
- Use signed manifest uploads when a cloud agent holds the artifact but cannot install the CLI.
- Mask claim tokens, upload tokens, API keys, and auth JSON in job logs.
- Do not persist personal `~/.spacefast/auth.json` in ephemeral CI runners.

### Mint A CI Deploy Key

The full bootstrap end to end: device login mints a human-approved session, then that session
mints a narrowly-scoped key for the CI credential. Never hand CI the personal session token.

1. Run device login (above) to get an interactive `SPACEFAST_TOKEN`: `POST /v1/auth/device`,
   show `data.verificationUrl`/`data.userCode`, poll `/v1/auth/device/poll` until `approved`,
   and capture `data.apiKey.secret` from that response. It is returned once, on the first
   approved poll only ‚Äî every later poll reports status `consumed`, not the secret again.
2. With that token active, mint the CI-scoped key: `sf api-keys create --name <ci-name>
   --preset ci_deploy`. Default to `ci_deploy` (publish-only); reach for `team_admin` only
   when the automation genuinely needs full team access such as domains, billing, or members.
3. Do not add `--json` to that create call. `--json` masks the one-time secret
   (`data.apiKey.secret` becomes a placeholder, the same masking `sf api-keys list` always
   shows). The real secret only appears in the human-readable `Secret: ...` line, and only on
   this one run.
4. Pipe the secret straight into the CI secret store without echoing it to the terminal or a
   log:

   ```bash
   sf api-keys create --name ci-deploy --preset ci_deploy \
     | grep '^Secret: ' | cut -d' ' -f2- \
     | gh secret set SPACEFAST_TOKEN
   ```

   Swap `gh secret set` for the target CI provider's secret-store command; the constraint is
   the same everywhere: the secret must never land in captured shell history, a log, or chat.
5. In the CI job, send the stored secret as bearer auth (`Authorization: Bearer
   $SPACEFAST_TOKEN`) or as the `SPACEFAST_TOKEN` env var for `sf publish --json`, per the
   one-off-job guidance above.

## Command Reference

- `sf access` ‚Äî Manage access policy.
- `sf access ban` ‚Äî Ban an identity class space-wide.
- `sf access block` ‚Äî Block an IP, CIDR, country, or identity class for a while.
- `sf access block ls` ‚Äî List active TTL'd blocks.
- `sf access clear` ‚Äî Clear cloud access policy.
- `sf access connection create` ‚Äî Create an identity connection.
- `sf access connection ls` ‚Äî List identity connections.
- `sf access connection rm` ‚Äî Revoke an identity connection.
- `sf access effective` ‚Äî Show effective access policy.
- `sf access grant` ‚Äî Grant access to an identity class.
- `sf access logout-all` ‚Äî Revoke all visitor sessions.
- `sf access ls` (alias: access list) ‚Äî List effective access rules.
- `sf access rm` (alias: access remove, access delete) ‚Äî Remove a cloud access rule.
- `sf access service-token create` ‚Äî Create a service token.
- `sf access service-token ls` (alias: access service-token list) ‚Äî List service tokens.
- `sf access service-token revoke` (alias: access service-token rm) ‚Äî Revoke a service token.
- `sf access set` ‚Äî Replace cloud access policy.
- `sf access show` ‚Äî Show cloud access policy.
- `sf access simulate` ‚Äî Simulate a request against the access policy.
- `sf activity` ‚Äî Show activity events.
- `sf agents init` ‚Äî Write Spacefast AGENTS.md guidance.
- `sf analytics` ‚Äî Print runtime analytics.
- `sf annotations` ‚Äî Manage annotations.
- `sf annotations export` ‚Äî Export annotations.
- `sf annotations get` ‚Äî Show an annotation.
- `sf annotations list` (alias: annotations ls) ‚Äî List annotations.
- `sf annotations reopen` ‚Äî Reopen an annotation.
- `sf annotations reply` ‚Äî Reply to an annotation.
- `sf annotations resolve` ‚Äî Resolve an annotation.
- `sf api` ‚Äî Call the Spacefast API directly.
- `sf api-keys` ‚Äî Manage API keys.
- `sf api-keys create` (alias: api-keys add) ‚Äî Create an API key.
- `sf api-keys delete` ‚Äî Revoke an API key.
- `sf api-keys list` ‚Äî List API keys.
- `sf api-keys ls` ‚Äî List API keys.
- `sf api-keys revoke` ‚Äî Revoke an API key.
- `sf api-keys rm` (alias: api-keys remove) ‚Äî Revoke an API key.
- `sf build` ‚Äî Build and pack static output.
- `sf builds cancel` ‚Äî Cancel a build.
- `sf builds detect` ‚Äî Detect framework and build settings.
- `sf builds get` ‚Äî Show a build.
- `sf builds logs` ‚Äî Print build logs.
- `sf builds ls` (alias: builds list) ‚Äî List builds.
- `sf builds resume-upload` ‚Äî Refresh source archive upload.
- `sf builds retry` ‚Äî Retry a terminal build.
- `sf channels` ‚Äî Manage channels.
- `sf channels history` ‚Äî Show channel history.
- `sf channels ls` (alias: channels list) ‚Äî List channels.
- `sf continue` ‚Äî Continue publishing after claim.
- `sf create` ‚Äî Create a Spacefast project directory.
- `sf demo` ‚Äî Run Spacefast demos.
- `sf demo agent` ‚Äî Run the local agent/MCP dogfood demo.
- `sf deploy` ‚Äî Build and deploy to Spacefast (alias of `sf publish`).
- `sf deployments` ‚Äî Manage deployments (alias of `sf versions`).
- `sf deployments get` ‚Äî Show a deployment (alias of `sf versions get`).
- `sf deployments ls` ‚Äî List deployments (alias of `sf versions ls`).
- `sf deployments promote` ‚Äî Promote a deployment (alias of `sf promote`).
- `sf deployments rollback` ‚Äî Roll back to a deployment (alias of `sf rollback`).
- `sf design` ‚Äî Generate design files from DESIGN.md.
- `sf design generate` ‚Äî Generate theme.json/layout.html from DESIGN.md.
- `sf docs` ‚Äî Search the bundled docs (offline).
- `sf doctor` ‚Äî Diagnose Spacefast CLI setup.
- `sf domains` ‚Äî Manage domains.
- `sf domains add` (alias: domains create) ‚Äî Attach a domain.
- `sf domains check` ‚Äî Check a domain.
- `sf domains diagnostics` ‚Äî Inspect domain diagnostics.
- `sf domains dns` ‚Äî Manage domain DNS records.
- `sf domains dns add` (alias: domains dns create) ‚Äî Add a DNS record.
- `sf domains dns batch` ‚Äî Apply a DNS record batch.
- `sf domains dns capabilities` ‚Äî Show DNS capabilities.
- `sf domains dns export` ‚Äî Export the DNS zone.
- `sf domains dns ls` (alias: domains dns list) ‚Äî List DNS records.
- `sf domains dns refresh` ‚Äî Refresh DNS provider snapshot.
- `sf domains dns rm` (alias: domains dns remove, domains dns delete) ‚Äî Delete a DNS record.
- `sf domains dns update` ‚Äî Update a DNS record.
- `sf domains ls` (alias: domains list) ‚Äî List space domains.
- `sf domains nameservers` ‚Äî Show domain nameservers.
- `sf domains nameservers set` ‚Äî Set domain nameservers.
- `sf domains rm` (alias: domains remove, domains delete) ‚Äî Remove a domain.
- `sf domains search` ‚Äî Search domain names.
- `sf env` ‚Äî Manage env vars.
- `sf env export-template` ‚Äî Create a dotenv import template from platform config.
- `sf env import` ‚Äî Import variables from a .env file.
- `sf env ls` (alias: env list) ‚Äî List space variables.
- `sf env pull` ‚Äî Pull variables.
- `sf env rm` (alias: env remove, env delete) ‚Äî Delete a space variable.
- `sf env set` ‚Äî Set a space variable.
- `sf feedback` ‚Äî Send support feedback.
- `sf git` ‚Äî Manage repository connections.
- `sf git build` ‚Äî Build repository source.
- `sf git connect` ‚Äî Connect repository.
- `sf git disconnect` ‚Äî Disconnect repository.
- `sf git ls` ‚Äî Show repository connection.
- `sf git origin` ‚Äî Install Spacefast git remote.
- `sf git sync` ‚Äî Sync remote repository source.
- `sf git update` ‚Äî Update repository connection.
- `sf init` ‚Äî Create a Spacefast project.
- `sf inspect` ‚Äî Inspect a space by ID, slug, URL, or domain.
- `sf link` ‚Äî Link the current directory to a space.
- `sf login` ‚Äî Log in to Spacefast.
- `sf logout` ‚Äî Log out of Spacefast.
- `sf logs` ‚Äî Print runtime activity.
- `sf mcp` ‚Äî Run the Spacefast MCP server.
- `sf mcp install` ‚Äî Generate MCP client config.
- `sf open` ‚Äî Open a space in your browser.
- `sf operations` (alias: ops) ‚Äî Inspect async operations.
- `sf password clear` ‚Äî Clear space password protection.
- `sf password set` ‚Äî Set space password protection.
- `sf profiles` ‚Äî List provider profiles.
- `sf profiles rm` (alias: profiles remove, profiles delete) ‚Äî Remove a provider profile.
- `sf profiles set` ‚Äî Create or update a provider profile.
- `sf profiles use` ‚Äî Select the active provider profile.
- `sf promote` ‚Äî Promote a version to a channel.
- `sf publish` ‚Äî Publish files to Spacefast.
- `sf redeploy` ‚Äî Retry the latest build (alias of `sf builds retry`).
- `sf rollback` ‚Äî Roll back to a previous version.
- `sf routing` ‚Äî Routing utilities.
- `sf routing compute` ‚Äî Compute routing.
- `sf routing inspect` ‚Äî Inspect local routing files.
- `sf runtime` ‚Äî Manage runtime.
- `sf runtime status` ‚Äî Print runtime status.
- `sf setup` ‚Äî Generate setup instructions.
- `sf setup agent` ‚Äî Generate agent setup instructions.
- `sf share` ‚Äî Manage sharing.
- `sf share add` ‚Äî Invite a person by email.
- `sf share link` ‚Äî Manage share links.
- `sf share link copy` ‚Äî Print a share link's live URL.
- `sf share link create` ‚Äî Create a share link.
- `sf share link ls` (alias: share link list) ‚Äî List share links.
- `sf share link revoke` ‚Äî Revoke a share link.
- `sf share ls` (alias: share list) ‚Äî Show the sharing overview.
- `sf share requests` ‚Äî List pending access requests.
- `sf share requests approve` ‚Äî Approve an access request.
- `sf share requests deny` ‚Äî Deny an access request.
- `sf share rm` ‚Äî Revoke an invite.
- `sf skills` (alias: skills install) ‚Äî Install or update Spacefast agent skills.
- `sf skills doctor` (alias: skills doctor) ‚Äî Check Spacefast skill installation.
- `sf skills install` (alias: skills install) ‚Äî Install or update Spacefast agent skills.
- `sf skills status` (alias: skills doctor) ‚Äî Check Spacefast skill installation.
- `sf spaces` ‚Äî Manage spaces.
- `sf spaces add` (alias: spaces create) ‚Äî Create an empty space.
- `sf spaces claim` ‚Äî Claim an anonymous space.
- `sf spaces download` ‚Äî Download a space version.
- `sf spaces duplicate` ‚Äî Duplicate a space with a download-and-publish copy.
- `sf spaces export` ‚Äî Export a space archive.
- `sf spaces get` ‚Äî Show a space.
- `sf spaces import` ‚Äî Import a space archive.
- `sf spaces ls` (alias: spaces list) ‚Äî List spaces.
- `sf spaces rm` (alias: spaces remove, spaces delete) ‚Äî Delete a space.
- `sf spaces transfer` ‚Äî Transfer a space to another team.
- `sf spaces update` ‚Äî Patch space metadata.
- `sf status` ‚Äî Show CLI status.
- `sf switch` ‚Äî Switch teams.
- `sf sync` ‚Äî Sync pending changes.
- `sf teams` ‚Äî Manage teams.
- `sf teams accept` ‚Äî Accept a team invitation.
- `sf teams create` (alias: teams add) ‚Äî Create a team.
- `sf teams invitations` ‚Äî Manage team invitations.
- `sf teams invitations add` (alias: teams invitations create) ‚Äî Create a team invitation.
- `sf teams invitations cancel` ‚Äî Cancel a team invitation.
- `sf teams invitations ls` (alias: teams invitations list) ‚Äî List team invitations.
- `sf teams invitations resend` ‚Äî Resend a team invitation.
- `sf teams ls` (alias: teams list) ‚Äî List teams.
- `sf teams members` ‚Äî Manage team members.
- `sf teams members ls` (alias: teams members list) ‚Äî List team members.
- `sf teams members rm` (alias: teams members remove, teams members delete) ‚Äî Remove a team member.
- `sf teams switch` ‚Äî Set default team.
- `sf transfers cancel` ‚Äî Cancel a space transfer.
- `sf transfers confirm` ‚Äî Confirm a space transfer.
- `sf transfers get` ‚Äî Show a space transfer.
- `sf versions` ‚Äî Manage versions.
- `sf versions download` ‚Äî Download a version archive.
- `sf versions get` ‚Äî Show a version.
- `sf versions ls` (alias: versions list) ‚Äî List space versions.
- `sf versions rm` (alias: versions remove, versions delete) ‚Äî Delete a version.
- `sf whoami` ‚Äî Show the current Spacefast account.
- `sf zero` ‚Äî Manage Zero runtime apps.
- `sf zero auth` ‚Äî Manage Zero auth.
- `sf zero build` ‚Äî Build a Zero artifact manifest.
- `sf zero create` ‚Äî Create a Zero app.
- `sf zero db` ‚Äî Inspect or dump Zero database data.
- `sf zero db dump` ‚Äî Dump Zero database rows.
- `sf zero db list` (alias: zero db ls) ‚Äî List Zero database tables.
- `sf zero db migrate` ‚Äî Plan Zero database migrations.
- `sf zero deploy` ‚Äî Build and deploy a Zero app.
- `sf zero dev` ‚Äî Run local Zero dev server.
- `sf zero inspect` ‚Äî Inspect Zero runtime metadata.
- `sf zero logs` ‚Äî List Zero runtime logs.
- `sf zero new` ‚Äî Create a Zero app.
- `sf zero run-many` ‚Äî Run many local Zero dev servers.
- `sf zero token` ‚Äî Manage Zero tokens.
- `sf zero token create` (alias: zero token add) ‚Äî Create a Zero token.
- `sf zero token list` ‚Äî List Zero tokens.
- `sf zero token ls` ‚Äî List Zero tokens.
- `sf zero token revoke` ‚Äî Revoke a Zero token.
- `sf zero token rm` (alias: zero token remove, zero token delete) ‚Äî Revoke a Zero token.

For anything without command sugar, `sf api [METHOD] /v1/...` signs a raw request with the active
credentials and prints the raw `{data}`/`{error}` envelope.

## Space Files

Spacefast recognizes these files in the published directory:

- `_redirects` for redirects, rewrites, custom 404 rules, and external `200` proxy rules
- `_headers` for supported response headers and Basic Auth
- nearest `404.html`
- `sf.jsonc` for serving config (index, fallback, listing, meta, templates, theme) and the optional `space` link

Spacefast publishes static artifacts. Server-executable files are never run by Spacefast Runtime.

Use `_redirects` for proxying:

```text
/api/* https://api.example.com/:splat 200
```

Do not create `.spacefast/proxy.json`. Internal `200` destinations are rewrites; only absolute
external URL destinations with status `200` are proxy rules.

If the artifact depends on access control for privacy, fail closed: confirm access control before
upload. Do not publish it until password protection, `_headers` Basic Auth, or another requested
access control is confirmed for the target space, unless the user explicitly accepts that the
artifact may be public.

## Plan-Gated Behavior

Do not guess whether a workspace is on a free or paid plan. For ordinary public artifacts, publish
the user's intended static artifact, then read and report API/CLI/runtime diagnostics. For private
or access-controlled artifacts, use the fail-closed rule above before uploading content.

When a feature is plan-gated:

- Say exactly what the diagnostic says.
- Use this plain wording when explaining gates: "Not available on Free. Available on Personal and Work."
- For external proxy routes, keep the `_redirects` rule if the user asked for it, but explain that
  the runtime may serve a platform page until the space has the required plan.
- For `_headers` Basic Auth, suggest `sf password set` as the simple gated-access alternative when
  the user does not specifically need file-based Basic Auth.
- For large files, report the blocked paths and size diagnostics. Do not rewrite or delete user
  files unless the user asks.
- For permanent Version URLs on Free, share the live URL first. Mention Version URL availability
  only when the publish receipt or diagnostics include it.

## Product Rules

Use Spacefast's user-facing nouns consistently:

- `space` is the main object.
- `publish` is the canonical action; `deploy` is an alias of `publish`. Pass `--build` to run
  the detected install and build commands before publishing the output.
- `version` is an immutable snapshot.
- `domain` is user-facing; avoid `hostname` except for DNS diagnostics.
- `access token`, `claim token`, and `claim link` have distinct meanings.

Default to correctness over speed. A publish is complete only when the API receipt or CLI command
returns successfully.
